g.data-narration: Welcome to CPU Scheduling Algorithms. We'll explore how operating systems decide which process runs next.
CPU scheduling is needed to maximize CPU utilization through multiprogramming, enabling multiple processes to share the CPU.
g.text: • Multiprogramming: Maximize CPU utilization by switching between processes
• Time-sharing: Allow multiple users to interact simultaneously
• Fairness: Ensure all processes get fair CPU time

g.data-narration: We evaluate scheduling algorithms using five key criteria: CPU utilization, throughput, turnaround time, waiting time, and response time.
First-Come, First-Served is the simplest algorithm. Processes are executed in the order they arrive in the ready queue.
g.text: • Simplest algorithm - processes scheduled in arrival order
• Non-preemptive: Once CPU allocated, process keeps it until done
• Can lead to Convoy Effect: short processes wait behind long ones

g.data-narration: Let's calculate waiting time for FCFS. P1 waits 0ms (first), P2 waits 24ms, P3 waits 27ms. Average is 17ms.
Shortest-Job-First selects the process with the smallest CPU burst. It provides optimal average waiting time but is hard to implement.
g.text: • Selects process with smallest next CPU burst
• Optimal for minimizing average waiting time
• Difficult to implement - need to predict next CPU burst
• Can cause Starvation of long processes

g.data-narration: In this SJF example with arrival times, processes are executed in order of shortest burst: P1, P2, P4, P3 giving average waiting time of 7.75ms.
Priority Scheduling assigns a priority to each process. Higher priority processes run first. It can cause starvation of low-priority processes.
g.text: • Each process assigned a priority, highest priority runs first
• Priority can be internal (time limits, memory) or external (user-defined)
• Starvation: Low priority processes may wait indefinitely
Solution: Aging - gradually increase priority of waiting processes

g.data-narration: Round Robin is designed for time-sharing. Each process gets a small time quantum, typically 10-100ms, then gets preempted.
g.text: • Each process gets a time quantum (10-100ms), then preempted
• Preemptive: Process interrupted if not complete in quantum
• Large quantum → FCFS behavior | Small quantum → high context switch overhead

g.data-narration: With quantum=4ms, processes cycle through: P1(0-4), P2(4-7 done), P3(7-10 done), then P1 continues. Average waiting time is 5.67ms.
Multilevel Queue partitions the ready queue into separate queues based on process type, each with its own scheduling algorithm.
g.text: • Ready queue partitioned into separate queues
• Based on process characteristics: system, interactive, batch processes
• Each queue can have its own scheduling algorithm
• Scheduling between queues: fixed priority or time slice

g.data-narration: Multilevel Feedback Queue is the most flexible - processes can move between queues based on their CPU burst behavior.
g.text: • Most flexible: processes move between queues
• Based on behavior and CPU burst characteristics
• Parameters: number of queues, algorithms per queue, upgrade/demote rules
• More CPU-intensive processes get demoted, interactive ones get promoted

g.data-narration: Modern OS use hybrid approaches: Windows uses priority-based with dynamic adjustment, Linux uses CFS, and macOS uses multilevel feedback queues.
Key comparison: FCFS is simple but causes convoy effect; SJF has optimal waiting time but hard to implement; Round Robin is good for time-sharing.
Remember these exam tips: practice Gantt charts, waiting time = turnaround - burst time, SJF gives minimum average waiting time.
g.text: • Gantt Charts: Practice drawing for all algorithms
• Waiting Time = Turnaround Time - Burst Time
• SJF gives minimum average waiting time
• Watch for starvation in SJF & Priority
🎯 Master these concepts for your exams!
